data.title: Walmart: Great Execution, Terrible Entry Point (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Thesis

Walmart Inc. (WMT) is making an aggressive push into AI, rolling out tools like its assistant Sparky to squeeze more efficiency out of a sprawling operation that runs on thin margins. It's already helping the company work more efficiently. Last quarter, Walmart’s operating income increased along with sales growth, and its e-commerce business became profitable. With these improvements, Walmart is putting itself in a stronger position to compete with Amazon in retail.

Be that as it may, I am taking a very bearish view. The market is pricing this stock as if it has already executed a flawless AI transformation and successfully fended off Amazon. That’s unrealistic. There are real risks here. Integrating AI is difficult and expensive, and Amazon is relentless. None of that appears to be reflected in the current price.

The Spark Powering Walmart Inc.'s Retail Reinvention

As February 19 approaches and Walmart prepares to report Q4 earnings, I think there’s a bigger story here. Specifically, who controls the next phase of retail profitability in North America? And right now, Walmart is making a serious run at territory long dominated by Amazon (AMZN). Not with price cuts. Not with store openings. But with agents. AI agents.

Last quarter, Walmart increased its operating income by 8% even though sales only grew by 5.9%. That shows the company is becoming more efficient and making more profit from each dollar of sales. And if Walmart’s Q4 results match Wall Street expectations, EPS of $0.72 and revenue of $188.46 billion, it would confirm that this improvement is continuing. It would also mean Walmart is closing the profitability gap with Amazon, whose North American retail business reported a 9% operating margin in its most recent quarter.

One example of how it intends to get there is via Sparky, Walmart’s generative AI shopping assistant. On one level, it's kind of like a simple shopping alarm clock. For example, if a family buys the same items every week, Sparky picks up on that. Then it reminds them to reorder those staples automatically. But it also builds smart bundles. Basically, it pairs everyday essentials with related add-ons customers might want. So if you’re buying the usual basics, it suggests complementary products at the same time.

Okay, so if you're an avid Amazon user like I am, this all probably seems vaguely familiar and not that all revolutionary. I can’t remember the last time Amazon didn’t suggest similar or related products when I was about to buy something. It’s always showing items that match or goes well with what I’m looking at. And honestly, for Walmart, if that similar strategy works, then it makes sense to improve it even more, which is exactly the point I’m trying to make.

In Walmart's case, it's all about the upsell opportunities. When I first got into the retail side of finance, I always remembered my manager's sales mantra: "Would you like extra fries with that?" Although that was more about offering an annuity or mutual fund, back when those were more en vogue.

That mindset hasn’t disappeared; it’s just evolved. Sparky is built into Walmart’s four super agents framework. This means that customer-facing tools like Sparky are connected to Walmart’s behind-the-scenes systems. Together, they help optimize everything from inventory forecasting to associate workflows.

In supply chains, AI agents help predict customer demand and change delivery routes in real time. This reduces waste and cuts down on costly mistakes that used to hurt profit margins. Meanwhile, merchant dashboards chew through mountains of data and spit out recommendations so teams can push out new products without waiting on a room full of analysts to catch up. For example, Walmart’s Trend-to-Product system shortens fashion production cycles by up to 18 weeks. This allows Walmart to spot trends earlier, turn them into products more quickly, and sell them with less leftover inventory.

And that’s where the flywheel begins to spin faster. A recent survey shows that 81% of Walmart shoppers use Sparky to check product details and see if items are in stock before making a purchase. This high level of use shows strong customer engagement, which helps increase the chances that shoppers actually complete their purchases. At the same time, the company’s retail media division grew by 33% compared to the previous year. This growth was 6x faster than the company’s overall revenue growth. It now accounts for about one-third of Walmart’s operating income. A large portion of that growth is boosted by sponsored prompts that are built directly into Sparky’s conversations. Once you wire the place for execution, it spreads. On store floors, AI tells associates what to do first. Online, the goal is to move e-commerce from 19% of revenue last fiscal year to 50% within five years.

As it stands, over 50% of Walmart's e-commerce fulfillment volume now runs through automated centers, cutting shipping costs by double digits for seven consecutive quarters. Last quarter, CEO McMillon also indicated that, thanks to AI agents, they enabled same-day delivery for 35% of digital orders. It's only going to improve from here.

All of this mirrors Amazon's robotics push, deployed with Walmart's scale. It's a machine swarm of fulfillment, yielding 40% better unit productivity. That stands in contrast to Amazon’s $200 billion capex splash on AI infrastructure, something I recently covered when upgrading Amazon to a Strong Buy. But that's internal spend for Amazon. In parallel, Walmart’s strategy focuses heavily on partnerships with Google Gemini and OpenAI. By working closely with these companies, Walmart reduces how much it needs to spend on building everything itself, while still benefiting from the growth. At least on the surface, the strategy appears to be delivering. Last quarter, Walmart’s e-commerce sales increased by 27%. Even more importantly, its e-commerce business became profitable for the first time. This is a major shift from previous quarters, when online operations were losing money and putting pressure on overall profit margins.

In all fairness, Profitero’s 2025 U.S. Price Wars study showed that Amazon still had the lowest online prices among major U.S. retailers, which is why it's still my first go-to when shopping online, with prices about 14% lower on average than other large online competitors. While Walmart was closer than most other competitors to Amazon in price, it is narrowing that gap to about 4% higher on average than Amazon’s price. Better, yes, just not good enough.

Looking ahead, management thinks that agentic commerce and AI-powered personalization will help drive long-term growth, rather than immediately boosting profit margins. For the full year, operating income is expected to grow between 4.8% and 5.5%. As for Q4, the company expects stronger growth of 8% to 11%. Of course, there's always risks. If Walmart’s supply chain AI struggles during periods of unpredictable demand, it'll hurt performance. Also, relying too heavily on partnerships could limit how much control Walmart has over its own data. That might weaken its ability to compete with Amazon and its $142 billion AWS juggernaut.

A Richly Valued Walmart

Right now, WMT’s blended P/E ratio is currently 50.44x, according to FAST Graphs. Just on optics, if I didn't know this chart represented Walmart, I would've guessed some kind of small or mid-cap meme tech stock. That said, WMT's historical average P/E is 19.49x, well above a conservative fair value level of 15x.

Looking at the forecasts, its estimated fair value's way below, around $40. Needless to say, it's overvalued on these measures alone. Meanwhile, expected earnings growth is projected at just 9.84% per year, which is relatively modest. So, in theory, if the P/E ratio falls back toward its historical average, investors could face an estimated return of -25.56%.

Looking at another measure, Walmart’s EV/EBITDA ratio is 26.44x. That too is much higher than the sector median of 11.88x, a 122% premium. For me, this raises a real question about whether the stock has run too far, too fast, and whether it can actually hold up from here. Especially if the macro backdrop gets tougher. As for a deeper dive on that topic, check out SA analyst's Jack Bowman's take on how "The Consumer Is Tapping Out."

If there were a margin of safety hiding somewhere else in the financials, I'd hope to expect to find it in cash flow. But that’s not the case either. Cash flow growth projections suggest EPS could grow between 5% and 12% annually through 2029. However, Walmart’s price-to-cash flow ratio is 25.99x, compared to the sector’s 11.64x. That's another large premium to contend with. And with an extremely low dividend yield, it doesn't give you much of a cushion either.

Final Verdict

Strong sell. Normally, Walmart’s convenience, ubiquitous footprint, massive scale, and long-standing household brand would justify me giving it some extra flexibility in a final rating. But the current valuation numbers are simply too extreme, even absurd. And while Walmart’s AI-driven margin improvements do show great promise, the stock is trading at 50x blended earnings, roughly 3x its historical average. At this level, the price already bakes in very optimistic expectations heading into earnings next week.

data.title: Belden Q4: The Supercycle Is Going To Lift Everyone, But Be Cautious
data.description:
Introduction

Belden Inc. (BDC) just reported Q4 and full-year numbers, so I thought I’d go through them in more detail and give some comments on its outlook and why I think we will see further upside ahead.

By the Numbers

Looking at the headline numbers, sales for Q4 came in at $720m, up around 8% y/y, beating analysts’ estimates by around $23m. The breakdown of revenue segments shows us that revenues increased 5% organically, with Automation Solutions up 10% to $2.715B, while Smart Infrastructure came in flat. For the full year, revenues were up 10% and 6% organically, with AS leading the charge with 11% growth, while SIS was up 1%.

So, the clear revenue driver is the Automation solutions, while SIS lagged quite a bit and dragged the company’s overall performance. The automation surge can be attributed to some tailwinds in efficiency, higher solutions penetration, and decent vertical strength. The management actually said during the call that high inflation and labor shortages benefited the company. Also, solutions-based wins accounted for over 20% of the segment’s revenue, with strong demand in the energy, discrete manufacturing, and mass transit sectors also playing a role. The weak broadband and telco sector didn’t help the SIS segment, as inventory overhang, the transition between technologies from older DOCSIS standards to newer fiber, and the slump in commercial real estate played the main roles in such a sluggish performance overall.

Moving on to the company’s profitability and efficiency, Q4 non-GAAP EPS came in at around $2.08, beating estimates by 13 cents, so it was a decent beat, and is up 23% y/y. A record for the company. Adjusted EBITDA came in at $459m, an increase of 12%, with an adjusted EBITDA margin coming in at 16.9%, up 20bps compared to the prior year. Adjusted EPS for the year came in at $7.54, up 19%. So, we can see that the company is quite a bit more profitable this time around. The shift in mix towards the higher-margin solutions, like the integrated packages that come with hardware and software, helped the company achieve higher profitability.

Let’s take a look at the company’s financial situation. BDC ended the year with around $390m in cash and equivalents and almost $1.3B in long-term debt. How worrisome is this? For the full year, the company’s interest expense was around $46m, while GAAP operating income was around $315m, so its interest coverage ratio is around 7x, which is quite healthy and well above analysts’ requirements of at least 2x. Additionally, the company’s operating cash flow was around $355m, and free cash flow was around $219m. With over $390m in cash and good cash flow performance, this debt is not an issue.

The management was kind enough to provide us with some color on the next quarter’s performance. Sales at the midpoint should be around $682.5m, implying around 9.2% y/y growth, which came in above consensus estimates of around $661.2m. Adjusted EPS should be in the range of $1.65-$1.75, above consensus expectations of $1.68, if we take the midpoint to be $1.70.

We can see that investors liked the double beat and the surprisingly strong guidance for the next quarter. The company’s share price reached new all-time highs on the day, going up as much as to reach $159.99, but due to market jitters since then, the company’s share price has come down slightly to around $150, which is still a good performance despite the wobbly market.

I liked the report. It showed us that the company is benefiting quite a bit from the high-demand end-markets like data centers, and the guidance showed us that the momentum is accelerating.

Comments on the Outlook

During the conference call, we could see where the management is looking to take the company going forward, and that is to follow the money. Enterprises are looking to spend a vast amount of dollars on upgrading their infrastructure, and BDC is looking to capitalize on this significantly by pivoting and focusing on the only growth story of the past few years that is somewhat exciting, the AI growth story and data centers. The CEO, Ashish Chand, mentioned that the AI data center pipeline more than doubled in 2025, which was still quite a small part of the total revenues; it is being looked at as one of the major revenue drivers for 2026, along with the BEAD program, which is a government-funded broadband expansion. The company expanded its AI data center by 300% as they see a lot of growth opportunity here, and BEAD funding will certainly flow in this year.

To support this pivot into being a complete solutions provider, the management decided that it would be best to restructure the company’s revenue segments and to eliminate them, as they believe that Smart Infrastructure was hiding high-growth areas like data centers, as the segment was grouped with slow-moving broadband. The goal now is to move to a “Unified Functional Model.” I’m still not sure how that is going to look. I’m very curious to see how the company is going to show this change in the next quarter’s report.

Clearly, like with many companies associated in some way with AI data centers, BDC is chasing the money, and that is fine with me. It looks like this is the only way to see any sort of improvements in profitability and top-line growth. We are witnessing right now, probably, one of the most lopsided economic expansions in tech history. AI infrastructure is not only growing faster than any other business segment, but it is currently the only segment that is in a super cycle, with many other legacy industries either stagnant or having some low single-digit growth. Analysts are seeing that companies aren’t just spending more money on data centers and AI infrastructure, but they are seeing that they are redirecting money away from general-purpose servers and legacy systems to fund AI clusters, calling it a Zero-Sum game. This cannibalization of capital is unprecedented, so obviously, you have to go where the money is. That means I can see the company’s share price continue to rise for much longer, unless the growth expected doesn’t materialize, in which case, the company’s share price will come back down to earth relatively quickly.

Should you invest now?

I think it is important to check the company’s intrinsic value to see what would be a good share price to start a position, given the promising growth outlook on the back of data center and AI growth.

Below are the assumptions I used.

For revenues, I am modelling that due to data center demand, the company will see a boost; however, it won’t be a massive boost since the data center revenues are still low compared to the rest of the revenues the company generates. The same for operating margins; I can see further profitability improvements because AI data center infrastructure demands higher margins, so the company’s profitability will improve. For the rest of the assumptions, I went with the company’s historical averages and a tax rate that is more aligned with a company that essentially doesn’t have any more tax credits and will be paying the full corporate tax rate in the U.S.

With the assumptions, we get the following EPS projections.

These are slightly above what analysts are expecting the company to do over the next few years, but I’m satisfied with these projections.

With that said, the company’s intrinsic value is around $150 a share, which means the company is trading at its fair value.

If you are looking for more exposure to the only growing business segment I mentioned, then I would say it is not a bad time to buy some shares in BDC. However, for me, I am not looking to add more concentration risk at the moment, as my portfolio continues to be overweighted in AI and data center growth stories. So, I am going to assign the company a Hold rating.

If you are happy with owning the company as it has already gone up over the last few years, there is no need to sell yet, as there will be more tailwinds ahead. Trimming a bit off the top is advisable, but leaving most of the position is probably a good idea. I’ve been trimming my best performer, but they keep climbing up, so they continue to be massive, and I believe that is going to go on for at least another couple of years, given how hyperscalers are looking to spend billions of dollars more on their AI dreams.

data.title: This Is What Your Friends And Relatives Will Say About The Market This Weekend
data.description:
The Noise-Producing Machine

When it comes to the weekend, I have a small advantage. Being based in Europe means that weekend talks start 6 to 9 hours ahead. Considering Friday nights, I have the advantage of knowing “in advance” how this weekend's conversations will probably sound. After all, until Thursday, we could talk about a bull market, but, as I am crafting this article, this year's gains have been erased, and the S&P 500 is down 0.50%, and the DOW didn't hold above 50,000. At the same time, the equal weight index (RSP) is up almost 5%, which is why I chose it as one of my 2026 picks when I said I was expecting the revenge of the S&P 493.

Well, guess what I have been discussing as the weekend begins? First of all, people are scared and say that the market is falling. In particular, “SaaSpocalypse” was one of the favorite topics. CapEx unsustainability came in second and sounded more like: Big Tech is in trouble. Some people who know a bit more in-depth how I invest wondered why safe havens such as S&P Global are plummeting and asked me to explain what is going on.

So, I am willing to bet that by the time my readers are having their brunch or will be hanging out with their friends or relatives, they will hear different versions of these ideas that are circulating:

  1. “AI is going to leave everyone jobless, and it will be a disaster.”
  2. “Why isn't the market up if the jobs report was good?”
  3. “The hyperscalers are going to get burned because they are wasting over $650B in capex.”
  4. “The market is plummeting, and it is better to buy gold or move to cash.”

This is what the noise-producing machine has chosen as its current output to fill everyone's ears and mouths with something to discuss so that people's attention is channeled in this direction.

Now, last year, when the market almost entered bear territory after Liberation Day, I wrote that we were before an opportunity. In general, I believe every time the market falls, the odds in favor of the long-term buyers increase.

However, we should not be too simplistic. We always have to anchor our decisions on what we read between the lines. After all, in 2022, the last bear market occurred as the economy was actually doing well, but investors feared that rising inflation would lead many central banks to hike interest rates so much that the economy would be thrown into a global recession. We know what happened.

Not being able to filter signals from noise has a cost, which, before we even need to calculate it as the cost of the opportunity we lost, can be described in a very effective binary way: regret or returns. Noise leads to regrets; signals help generate returns. Let's then audit the four main fears with some data.

The Signals

1. The AI Disruption Story And Jobs Data

I forgot to mention that this weekend began with a friend saying that my work as a financial analyst on Seeking Alpha will soon be dead because I will be replaced by AI. So, it seems I am directly involved in this process we are discussing. This story is damaging the software sector (IGV) with stocks such as ServiceNow (NOW), Adobe (ADBE), and Salesforce (CRM) all moving continually south. Even Microsoft (MSFT) has not been spared. Now, even though they are selling off, they are all reporting revenue and earnings growth, with steady subscription rates, low churn, and increasing backlog. Surely, it is too early to see the impact of agentic AI, even though most of these companies are all openly speaking about their internally developed agents, which should prevent their customers from moving away from their platforms.

Now, I think part of this disruption story is real, and JPMorgan's analysts rightly pointed out that IGV is now trading at the widest gap to the downside with its 200-d moving average since the dot-com bubble. The debate is clear: if the bears are correct, then we should exit software. But if this is an overreaction, we are before one of the best opportunities that the market is presenting us with. We are seeing that fears are spreading, with several websites even describing this as the sell-off metastatization, just to add a bit of color to the topic and explain why job sectors where human knowledge is required and applied to datasets (i.e., tax planning, financial services, and logistics data management).

I have no doubt that AI will change how we work, how we spend our time working, and how businesses will develop their models. There will be winners and losers, and I am not saying that companies such as Intuit (INTU) or S&P Global (SPGI) are immune to this change. But I think that shrinking the perimeter of our thought process due to fears hinders us in focusing on more relevant questions. For example, I think it is more interesting to understand whether AI will not only destroy companies (and their value) but also whether AI will redistribute value. Before software, many jobs worked differently. But before and after software, consultants, just to name a category that seems under attack, are needed. They simply changed the way they work and became more effective. We also thought that online sales would destroy retailers. Well, it is more accurate to say that we saw many retailers change and develop their business model, adapting to online sales. Moreover, I recently showed that, when we put together a cooling job market and a fast-growing GDP, the real explanation that the data gives us is that productivity is growing while hours worked are barely up. This is the productivity dividend that is driving margin expansion for many corporations, as we see in the graph below.

What we see below is of utmost importance: it needs to make us think that value is being created and that it can be redistributed. Moreover, the BLS January 2026 employment report surprised us with 130k new nonfarm payrolls and an unemployment rate that moved down to 4.3%. There has been some discussion about part-time jobs, with some who believed that the unemployment rate was low because part-time jobs were increasing. It is true that YoY they are up by 410k, but in January, the number of part-time jobs for economic reasons declined by 453k. When we look at the breakdown by sector, we see that healthcare, social assistance, and construction led the charge, creating 157k jobs, while the federal government jobs and financial activities reduced their payrolls by 34k and 22k, respectively. We should carefully consider the financial categories. After the Big 4 banks released their earnings, I explained why I thought they would be the first beneficiaries of AI. As a matter of fact, they did announce they would pause hirings and reduce headcount while guiding for higher margins. So, it is true that some jobs are being lost. But, in the meantime, we are not seeing industry disruption.

People who are not familiar with how the market often behaves were surprised that after the strong January report, the market turned negative. Well, this is because we are in an environment where good news is bad news and where bad news is good news. A labor market that holds up, while positive for workers, is negative, or supposedly so, for inflation. This means that the FED won't need to lower rates because unemployment is low while inflation is still above 2%. Interest rates are like gravity for the stock market. When they are high, the market plummets; when they move south, the market surges. As a matter of fact, investors are now pricing a very low chance of a rate cut in March.

Let's go back to software for a moment. Some of the names I mentioned above have created ecosystems that are deeply embedded in corporate workflows. This also allows these software companies to often have access to unique datasets that LLMs can't use. I think that we should be careful in identifying those companies that have big and useful proprietary datasets. This creates a first barrier to disruption. Secondly, many of these software companies are best positioned to understand what AI is about and to implement it within their ecosystems. I have heard many software CEOs recently say that as long as the AI agents they develop internally are helpful and effective enough to satisfy their customers, they see no real threat of companies dumping their software suites or investing to create their own internal platform. Switching costs matter. Moreover, think about this case. Let's imagine that a big Fortune 500 company cuts its ties with SAP or with Salesforce because it wants to develop its own custom platform, hiring 4 or 5 engineers (if Claude Cowork was done in a week and a half with the supervision of just a few people, this is the number we have to consider in our assumption) to create it. The main risk I see is that if 1 or 2 of these engineers then change jobs, the company may quickly lose grip on the development and maintenance process. Having a third-party provider places the risk on the platform developer. One could argue that in a few years, it will be the internally born AI platform that will be able to develop itself. It might be true, but I still believe there will need to be at least one or two human minds that know the business logic, its processes, and understand how to integrate a platform within the entire business of the company. Moreover, a company such as SAP has spent decades building connectors between the original platform and tens, if not hundreds, of internal systems and compliance frameworks that can't be replicated right away.

So, I think that the disruption story is real, but that it is probably mispriced. Proprietary datasets, switching costs, regulatory issues, and human accountability are part of what I would call a multi-layered stack of barriers to full destruction. This is why I am gradually moving toward the camp of those who believe that ServiceNow, SAP, and similar software companies are trading at extremely attractive multiples right now. The core idea is that switching costs make it almost impossible to disrupt these companies, although we may see them remodeled by AI.

2. CapEx Is Moat

People are worried about the unexpected capex surge that the four hyperscalers announced: $650B for this year alone. If we add Oracle, we are close to $750B. We have seen stocks such as Amazon and Microsoft rattled by the market, with investors dumping them. This is why I talked about a capex-geddon scenario. These numbers look crazy, and investors are worried about negative FCF (in the case of Amazon, it will almost be a guarantee) and earnings compression because of higher depreciation for several years to come. At the same time, these companies can afford these investments. Together, they have almost $421B in cash and cash equivalents. This means that they will take on new debt, but without endangering their balance sheets. Moreover, during the year, they should generate cash from operations of above $580B (their TTM total), which means that they will basically be able to self-fund this huge cycle, with debt being an optionality and not a need.

When we study the economics of antitrust and regulation, we learn that capex is often a barrier to entry, which causes big issues when regulators want more competition. Deploying $650B in a year distances the hyperscalers from the rest of the world, placing them in a league of their own where they have proprietary infrastructure and become the true utilities for the 21st century. The bears see this capex as waste, but I see it as extremely important capital allocation. Amazon already gave us the footprint. In 2021 and 2022, it invested heavily in logistics and warehouses; its FCF went negative, but then Amazon switched to harvesting mode, and we saw its FCF swing and move up above $50B. It would have inched higher, had AI not been released, fueling a newer and even more strategic investment cycle. The hyperscalers are building data centers not because they want to have more capacity for the future, but because demand for compute is already exceeding what they can fulfill. This means that, by building data centers and investing in AI infrastructure, they are securing agreements with their customers that can last for a decade or two.

There is a caveat. GPUs have a short lifecycle and after 3 to 5 years become obsolete. They can be repurposed, but they won't perform as well as the newer versions optimized with new software. I discussed this in depth last week, and allow me to quote myself on this issue, where I highlight that GPUs are only one variable of the equation:

not all CapEx is going toward GPUs. We are in a data center buildout, which implies that buildings and networks are also under construction. These will have a useful life that can be compared to that of a warehouse. I would consider that there are three main categories that are seeing massive capital deployment, where we can use three different time horizons. GPUs that are used for training have a shorter life cycle, followed by repurposed GPUs, which can have a couple of additional years. Then we have servers and their ancillaries (racks, cooling systems, and similar) that have a lifecycle of 7 to 10 years (you can read the Uptime Institute report on Expected Useful Life for data center equipment). Finally, we have the third category, which includes the facilities and the shell layer of each data center. Here we have a typical lifecycle of 15 to 20 years, with the real estate component that can last for 30 years.

3. The Market Is Not Falling, But Rotating

This could be the easiest, and yet, it is incredibly difficult to wipe people's fears away. I mean, the market is barely down YTD, and we are sitting on massive gains since the 2022 trough. The DOW is around 50,000. The S&P is still around 6,800. True, this year, the equal weight and the Russell (IWM) are outperforming the S&P 500, and this is because earnings breadth is improving, while the hyperscalers are being dumped. Actually, when breadth matures, it is usually a sign that a bull market is starting. By no means are we in a bear market, and even if one were to take place, we would still be well above the levels of the last two bear markets that took place in 2020 and 2022. If Big Tech has a slow year in the market, it is perfectly fine. I would call this a normalization or a breather, which sometimes is needed when we see stocks such as Meta or Alphabet that have surged rapidly from their lows. The stocks may underperform the market (although I doubt this will last for long), but their economics are improving. In the meantime, we could have the benefit of seeing a reduced concentration of the index, with the 493 stocks adding some gains and accounting for a larger portion of the index's earnings.

4. What January CPI Data Reveal

The BLS released the January 2026 CPI data, and we have probably seen in the headlines that inflation came in lower than expected. The U.S. CPI moved up 0.2%, while the consensus was expecting 0.3%, and on a YoY basis, we are at 2.4% while the consensus was 2.5% and the December report was at 2.7%. So, it appears that inflation is truly cooling. In particular, energy was down 1.5%, and this surely helps. It is true that inflation is ex-energy. But if energy is cheap, a major input cost for many other items becomes more affordable and more manageable. This is then reflected in the price for the consumer.

This report is important. First of all, we knew inflation was moderating, as the 12-month percent change in the CPI graph shows below.

But many were still expecting a hot print, forecasting that inflation would heat up once again as a consequence of tariffs or start-of-the-year price hikes. It seems that the trajectory inflation is following hasn't changed, as a gradual moderation toward the 2% goal now seems underway, with the long-term goal also considered truly achievable. Incidentally, we should note that we are moving in that direction, not under a soft landing scenario, but in years where GDP growth is strong. I think this further proves that a productivity dividend is here. Wages can increase without fueling risky spirals to the upside; companies can see their earnings and their margins increase; and the Fed can hold steady; these three facts can be explained only in one way: more output per worker is being produced. That's why I say that CapEx is not a waste of money: it is an investment that is already driving productivity up. The latest available numbers that we have are from 3Q2025, where they show that the nonfarm sector reached labor productivity growth of 4.9% YoY. This was the result of a very mild increase in hours worked of 0.5%. You then see for yourself that something is happening and is actually making workers able to produce more.

This leads me to my last consideration. Bears believe we are in a zero-sum game: growth requires inflation, AI destroys jobs, investments give up returns, and so on. I don't see it in this way. If productivity is increasing, we are in a value-creating environment, which explains why the country's GDP can grow while inflation is moderating, why massive capex investments can get along with margin expansion, why AI adoption can also lead to new job creation, and why a still-tight labor market goes along with an environment of price stability.

What To Tell Your Friends

This weekend, when you hear people saying that AI will make us all jobless, you could ask them if that happened when software was invented. But you could also ask them if they are aware of companies whose earnings are plummeting because of AI. You will have to leave open the possibility of unforeseen outcomes, but this weekend you will have the chance to address short-term noise with real data. In particular, growing productivity and higher margins are two clear signals that AI is creating value. This weekend, when you hear your relatives or friends go after the noise, use the signals and help them look ahead 5 to 10 years from now. Everyone will feel safer, and your weekend will be saved.

data.title: Dutch Bros Is Still One Of The Best Growth Stocks In The Restaurant Sector
data.description:
Dutch Bros (BROS) is still one of the restaurant sector stocks with the highest growth potential. While many restaurants are struggling right now, this coffee shop chain is still reporting high same-store sales growth and adding new locations. Now, Dutch Bros is selling food as well as coffee, so it has another way to generate additional revenue growth. Meanwhile, it continues to provide convenience and an entertaining experience to its guests. And now, once again, Dutch Bros has outperformed Wall Street analysts’ expectations and reported very strong quarterly results.

Dutch Bros closed the year 2025 with a great quarter. It posted 29.4% revenue growth and 7.7% same-shop sales growth, so not only is it adding new locations, but its existing locations are also selling more coffee. The company also reported that its adjusted EBITDA rose from $48.8 million to $72.6 million in the fourth quarter. With $443.6 million in revenue, Dutch Bros achieved a 16.4% adjusted EBITDA margin. Its full-year adjusted EBITDA margin was 18.5%.

This was a better result than I expected, and it was also a better result than other analysts expected, which is why Dutch Bros’ stock price rose 12% after hours on February 12. But even at a price of $57 per share, analysts might still be underestimating this coffee shop’s growth potential. I still don’t think Dutch Bros is as expensive as its forward P/E implies because it’s investing a lot of its income into growth right now.

Dutch Bros Plans to Continue Rapidly Opening Coffee Shops in the Medium Term

At the end of the year, this coffee shop chain had 1,136 locations, according to its Q4 earnings report. Now, it’s planning to have 2,029 locations by the end of 2029. That’s a 15.6% compound annual growth rate. Dutch Bros also plans to open at least 181 new locations in 2026, which is a 15.9% growth rate. So, Dutch Bros is guiding for nearly 16% growth from new locations alone over the next four-year period.

Dutch Bros isn’t a nationwide chain yet, and it hasn’t saturated the market in many regions where it does have coffee shops either. It has added some coffee shops in California, but it doesn’t have multiple coffee shops in many cities like Starbucks (SBUX) currently does. I haven’t seen pop-up Dutch Bros coffee shops inside grocery stores or big-box stores either, although Dutch Bros is more focused on drive-thru customers than Starbucks is. So I think Dutch Bros should be able to grow to 2,000 or more stores without running out of good locations. So even if its same-store sales were flat, this restaurant’s revenue would still grow by around 16% per year, although it’s likely to grow faster than that.

Dutch Bros Just Acquired Clutch Coffee

There are already small coffee shop chains in many regions where Dutch Bros could potentially expand. So Dutch Bros might be able to speed up its expansion by buying out its smaller competitors. In January 2026, Dutch Bros bought the coffee shop chain Clutch Coffee. This coffee shop’s business model was similar to Dutch Bros’ model. As Restaurant Dive explained, Clutch Coffee was also known for providing good customer service, and its employees were walking up to customers’ cars and bringing out their drinks. Clutch Coffee was also playing music at its coffee stands to entertain its customers.

On the Q4 2025 earnings call, Dutch Bros CEO Christine Barone explained that because this deal took place in 2026, the company has now added 20 of the 181 new locations that it plans to add this year. So the Clutch Coffee acquisition won’t change Dutch Bros’ expansion plans, although it did provide a convenient way for the company to get closer to its growth target. On the call, CFO Joshua Guenser also said, “Clutch provided us a great opportunity to grab a hold of 20 sites in a market that we were just moving into, to be able to enter that market relatively rapidly [in] a very capital-efficient way.”

So it sounds like Dutch Bros might make more acquisitions in 2026 or future years if it finds good deals. Otherwise, the company will probably just continue building new coffee shops itself. Either way, Dutch Bros’ growth plans don’t depend on finding smaller coffee shop chains to acquire. Technically, you could say this deal added inorganic revenue growth, but that doesn’t really matter because Dutch Bros could have just built 20 new coffee shops in North and South Carolina instead.

Dutch Bros Will Still Be Impacted By Higher Coffee Prices in 2026

In my previous article, I projected that Dutch Bros’ adjusted EBITDA margin would drop from 18% to 17.5% in 2025. This was partially because of the risk that coffee prices would remain elevated in 2026 due to climate change. Recently, coffee bean prices have dropped, but Dutch Bros still expects higher coffee bean prices to impact its margins in 2026. Additionally, Dutch Bros expects its margins to be lower because many of its coffee shops are now serving food. So, because of multiple factors, Dutch Bros’ CFO guided for the company’s adjusted EBITDA margin to drop 60 basis points in 2026. But the company also reported a stronger margin than I expected in Q4 2025, and its full-year adjusted EBITDA margin was 18.5%. So if its margin drops to 17.9% in 2026, that’s still higher than my original estimate.

Expanded Beverage Menus at Fast-Food Restaurants Are a Risk Factor

Fast-food restaurants such as McDonald’s (MCD) and Taco Bell (YUM) are planning to serve more blended drinks in 2026. Dutch Bros’ management didn’t seem to be worried about competitors on the call, but I still think competitors’ product launches could create additional risks for the company. While McDonald’s shut down its stand-alone CosMc’s concept last year, it still sounds like energy drinks are selling well at its traditional restaurants. And Taco Bell is adding Live Mas cafes that serve beverages that aren’t on its regular menu. Additionally, Chick-fil-A added frosted sodas and floats to its menu in January. So the quick-serve restaurants see a multi-billion-dollar growth opportunity in beverages and may start selling more drinks that are similar to Dutch Bros’ drinks.

My Updated Valuation for Dutch Bros

Dutch Bros’ 2026 guidance sounds conservative to me. The company now expects 2026 revenue of $2.0 to $2.03 billion, which would be 23% growth in comparison to its 2025 revenue of $1.638 billion. It’s also calling for same-shop sales growth in the 3% to 5% range. In 2025, the company posted 28% revenue growth and 5.6% same-shop sales growth. And in my previous article, I projected that this company would report 26% revenue growth in 2026. Additionally, Dutch Bros has a history of beating revenue and EPS estimates.

So, for 2026, I’ll project that Dutch Bros’ revenue will rise 26% to $2.064 billion instead. With a 17.9% adjusted EBITDA margin, it would report $369 million in EBITDA. It’s more complicated to calculate Dutch Bros’ EV than it is for other companies because of its complex share structure. I explained how this works in my previous article, and I’ll explain it again here if you didn’t read that article. According to its Q4 2025 report, Dutch Bros has 127.4 million diluted shares, as well as OpCo Class A units that can be converted into 50.5 million common shares. So, its real fully diluted share count is 177.9 million shares. Additionally, the non-controlling shareholders who own Dutch Bros’ OpCo Class A units have $217 million in equity.

So Dutch Bros’ market cap is really $57 * 177.9 million shares, or $10.140 billion. The company also has $269 million in cash, according to its Q4 2025 report. If I add its $852 million in lease liabilities and $196 million in long-term debt from the report, it has $1.048 billion in total debt. So, if I add this debt and subtract the value of Dutch Bros’ cash and its OpCo Class A units, I get an EV of $10.702 billion. Dutch Bros reported EBITDA of $260.4 million in 2025, so its current TTM EV/EBITDA is 41. This is still higher than Starbucks and McDonald’s TTM EV/EBITDA ratios, which are 25 and 19, respectively.

If I project that Dutch Bros’ EV will fall to 38 in 2026, the company’s EV would then be $14.022 billion. After subtracting $1.048 billion in debt and adding back $269 million in cash and $217 million in non-controlling shareholders’ equity, Dutch Bros’ market cap would be $13.460 billion. After dividing this figure by 177.9 million shares, I get a one-year target price of $75.66. That’s 33% higher than Dutch Bros’ current price of $57 per share.

Conclusion

I still think Dutch Bros’ stock is undervalued in comparison to its growth potential. Dutch Bros is still adding new coffee shops at a rapid pace while maintaining higher same-store sales growth than other restaurant chains. And it sounds like it can maintain this rapid expansion pace for at least the next 4 years as well. While its margins are likely to weaken a bit in 2026, it seems like short-term headwinds from higher coffee bean prices are the main reason for that. So I say that Dutch Bros is still a buy.

data.title: Shell: Integrated Gas Is In Demand
data.description:
Shell (SHEL) is one of the largest oil companies in the world, with an almost 4% dividend yield and a commitment to aggressively repurchasing shares. That, combined with strong natural gas and renewable portfolios, makes Shell a valuable investment. (We last wrote about it in December here.)

Shell History

Shell had $3.3 billion in adjusted earnings in the quarter, along with $9.4 billion in CFFO.

The company spent $21 billion in capex for the year, almost 10% of its market capitalization, showing a strong continued investment within its business. Removing the quarterly capex, the company had $4.2 billion in FCF, a sign of the tough environment, and a roughly 8% annualized FCF yield.

Shell managed to maintain a 4% dividend increase, with a 3.6% annualized yield. The company has started an aggressive buyback program, which we're a big fan of, with >$3 billion in buybacks for the 17th consecutive quarter. The company distributed 52% of its CFFO in 2025, a double-digit shareholder distribution the company could comfortably afford.

Shell is targeting 40-50% CFFO through the cycle, with a higher rate in 2025 as a result of lower prices, but a much more manageable rate in 2023-2025. The company expects to continue to find cost efficiencies, as expected, along with capex sitting in the $20-22 billion range.

The company's capex is expected to comfortably lead to FCF growth with >10% per-annum FCF growth/share through 2030. This is expected to be supported by ~$12 billion of annualized buybacks at the minimum, along with the growth from the company's investments. That combination, along with a low current valuation, shows Shell's strength.

Shell Results

Shell has faced a difficult macro environment for its business.

Oil prices have been trending down for the last few years. Gas prices have mostly recovered from the Ukraine-Russia invasion impact even with the increase of data center demands. Refining and chemical margins have recovered from weakness, but they still remain quite weak overall from a multi-year historical perspective.

The company's financial picture shows some debt growth to a 20% net-debt yield versus market cap. The company still managed to earn $26.1 billion in FCF for the year, a double-digit FCF yield, and $18.5 billion in adjusted earnings, putting it at a P/E of ~12. For the current weak prices, those are relatively strong earnings.

Shell Growth

Shell is focused on using its capital investment, not just to maintain its portfolio, but to ramp up its production.

The company has been pushing uptime, with upstream uptime approaching 90% and LNG uptime dipping recently, but staying similarly high. Shell's new projects are expected to deliver more than 1 million barrels/day by 2030, from a wide variety of projects. This includes a number of mega projects like the Qatar North Field development.

Shell Returns

Overall, Shell's focus is to continue long-term returns.

The company has maintained a strong commitment to shareholder returns, with a 3.6% dividend yield or $8.1 billion/year. At the same time, the company has remained committed to its more than $3 billion in quarterly buybacks metric it started, for more than $20 billion in annualized shareholder returns.

For the year, the company could afford that, but with weak prices going into 4Q 2025, the company's $4.2 billion in ending FCF had an impact on continued returns.

A weak environment with $45 billion in net debt could hurt continued long-term returns. However, with a modernized integrated gas portfolio and the start-up of exciting projects like Qatar's North Field, could hurt future shareholder returns.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is crude oil prices. Shell is a great investment at its current valuation, with continued growth potential at current oil prices. However, with trade wars and the potential for deals adding back Venezuela and Iran's supply, that could reduce prices and hurt the ability to continue shareholder returns.

Conclusion

Shell has remained one of the most steadfast of the major oil companies with its shareholder repurchase program. Not only does the company maintain a 3.6% dividend yield, but it's committed to its more than $3 billion/quarter dividend yield for more than 4 years now. That commitment is strong, high-single-digit shareholder returns.

At the same time, Shell is investing in a number of mega growth projects. These projects can add more than 1 million barrels/day in production by 2030, and the company is continuing to invest heavily in LNG, which is a very profitable and well-positioned transition fuel. Overall, that makes Shell a valuable investment.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: AI Ambitions And Trade Frictions, What's Next For Canadian Stocks?
data.description:
Canadian stocks outperformed their U. S. peers last year, but could that continue as investors re-assess the potential impact of AI and continued trade tensions? Michael O’Brien, Co-Head of Canadian Equity Portfolio Management, TD Asset Management, joins MoneyTalk to discuss opportunities and expectations for Canadian stocks.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell: The TSX has continued its outperformance against the S&P 500 so far this year, with the big Canadian tech stocks coming under pressure recently. Can that run continue? Joining us now to discuss is Michael O'Brien, co-head of Canadian Equity Portfolio Management with TD Asset Management.

Michael, always great to have you on the program, especially when we get a lot of Canadian earnings to sift through.

Michael O’Brien: Yeah, it's been a busy week. Thanks for having me on.

Greg Bonnell: Okay. So we have had this outperformance continuing from last year. As I take a look today, we're not having a great day. Shopify (SHOP), some of the other-- I guess we've seen as AI plays in this country-- have definitely been underperforming. How should Canadian investors be thinking about this?

Michael O’Brien: Well, I think if you look at the composition of the Canadian market and compare it to the composition of the US market, if we're comparing why would Canada outperform, underperform, I think what you need to realize about the S&P 500 in the US, roughly 5% of their index would be energy and materials combined. Whereas, to give you a sense, Apple (AAPL) is 6%, Microsoft (MSFT) is 5%. So those one tech stock is the equivalent of the entire energy materials composition of the market.

Come to Canada, energy and materials are about a third of our market-- so, vastly different. Right now, the big pain point year to date has been technology. And two drivers, really, one of them is more relevant for Canada than the other. The one that's not as relevant to Canada but is a big, big deal is investors' anxiety around the CapEx spending at the big hyperscalers-- Google (GOOGL), Meta (META), Microsoft (MSFT)-- astronomical CapEx numbers coming out.

And so, there's a growing anxiety among the investors that, OK, are you spending this because you want to, or are you spending because you need to? When are we going to see a return on this? I just happen to notice all your free cash flow's gone. So that's the one big thing that's been unnerving investors.

The second thing which has had that spillover into the Canadian market, and you alluded to it earlier, this role of artificial intelligence as a disruptive force. So AI, artificial intelligence-- we seem to be in this rolling wave of panics, where what will AI disrupt next? Which business model? Which industry? And software, obviously, has been in the teeth of this. But it's not just confined to software, it sort of spreads around from day to day. But that's been the second big debate or the big issue that's really been holding up the technology space.

And because technology is such a more important role, a bigger component of the US market, the Americans feel the pain more than we do. A lot of investors who had been quite happy owning these Big Tech behemoths and real juggernauts, as they start to get unnerved and they look for a new home for their money-- if you're taking a little bit out of Microsoft, which is a 5% position, and trying to put that across all of the stocks in the energy materials space, it's kind of like you pour a little bit of the bath water into a measuring cup. It doesn't take much to spill over. So I think that's what we're seeing.

And so in Canada, I think we've been benefiting from the fact that people are running away from technology. There's not a lot of technology to run away from in Canada. And there's a whole lot of commodity exposure that they can flee to, where people are thinking, well, chances are AI isn't going to disrupt a copper mine. AI isn't going to disrupt a natural gas well. So, I think that's what's been going on under the surface.

And so as long as that, I would call it an AI shakeout in technology, continues to be the dominant narrative, the Canadian market looks like a relatively better place to be.

Greg Bonnell: When I think about the AI disruption, they might be disrupting or potentially-- we don't know the extent, perhaps this is a bit overdone. But the AI is still there doing the disrupting. The AI is energy-hungry. We have things in Canada that could probably help feed the world's energy needs.

Michael O’Brien: Yep. Yeah, absolutely. I mean, if you just think about Cameco (CCJ) in the nuclear space. I mean, nuclear power is seen as one of the potentials, one of the components to solving this need for power for the big data centers. Cameco is a global leader. So, that's a great example of that.

We've got tons of natural gas up in Western Canada looking for a home. You see speculation about data centers, potentially AI-driven data centers in Western Canada-- lots of natural gas to fuel it. So you're absolutely right. There are a lot of potential beneficiaries.

Greg Bonnell: So this might be one of those stories. You look back in a couple of months and say, remember the big, bouncy action in the markets because of all the AI anxieties as we came out of winter? But then we're going to head into-- or we're in the middle of it right now-- the trade irritants don't go away. We're going to have to renegotiate the USMCA, CUSMA, at some point. How important is that to the Canadian market?

Michael O’Brien: Well, I think the USMCA renegotiation, revisiting that-- I think that's going to be the defining thing for the Canadian market this year. I think it's hugely important not just for the Canadian market but especially the Canadian economy that, hopefully-- I suspect this is going to be a very bumpy, rocky journey. I think most people have reconciled themselves to that. But I--

Greg Bonnell: I thought the Gordie Howe Bridge was part of all this, until it was--

Michael O’Brien: Who would have seen that coming? It seems to get more ridiculous every time you read a headline. Unfortunately, I think that's only going to accelerate until we do get to a deal. So my hope here is that if we can land a deal at some point in 2026, that would do a huge amount to remove a lot of uncertainty that's hanging over business makers' decisions in terms of where to spend that capital, which workers to hire, which factories to build. So, in my opinion, this is going to be a pivotal thing for the Canadian economy in 2026. If we can come to some sort of a reasonable landing spot on USMCA, then I think that's going to be a real positive for Canada.

Greg Bonnell: Is there a house view of us eventually finding a path here? It'll be bumpy along, or is it getting harder and harder now to be optimistic that--

Michael O’Brien: Yeah.

Greg Bonnell: So it will be dramatic, but we'll get through the other side.

Michael O’Brien: I think that's still the prevailing view, which then you worry about, am I being too sanguine about this? Am I too complacent about this? Because I don't think it would be a stretch of the imagination to see these things going off the rails, at least for a time. But I think everybody has been trained to believe that-- not out of a concern for Canada's well-being but more from just blatant self-interest-- that the US and the Trump administration, whether they want to admit it or not, they do need a trade deal.

They can't afford to-- with affordability being the number one issue heading into the US midterms, blowing up the USMCA and putting tariffs on however many goods coming across the border does not seem to be a way to address that. So, I think just from sheer self-interest, the cards do favor Canada-- that we will arrive at a reasonable, if not amicable, solution to this.

Greg Bonnell: We put all of that together, are we going to be sitting here in December or January of early next year saying, wow, look at us outperforming the Americans again? What are the odds of that?

Michael O’Brien: I think it's a pretty reasonable possibility. It's a pretty reasonable possibility here. Like I say, if we're into a period of time where the real economy is beginning to get off the mat-- and it seems to be. Some of the more recent labor market and PMI data out of the US is encouraging.

We know there's a lot of money coming from the Canadian spending on both defense and a lot of infrastructure projects. We know there's a lot of spending in the United States for tax cuts and business tax credits. All of that stimulus should be lifting the North American economies. And in that kind of environment, I think the strengths of the Canadian market lie in building and moving things.

Original Post

data.title: January CPI: Disinflation Surprise
data.description:
The BLS released consumer prices for January 2026 and revealed that their data says that prices disinflated once again; that is, that the rate of change is falling and continuing to fall since its relative peak over the summer and is now at 2.4% YoY, according to the BLS.

It's not just energy prices, which have been a culprit of inordinately low inflation readings in the past year, as the core level, which excludes food and fuel, also fell this month, after being steady since November—when the data kicked back on following the government shutdown.

The January CPI Report

Here is the report vs. estimates, for reference:

BLS Estimate
CPI MoM +0.2% +0.3%
CPI YoY +2.4% +2.5%
Core CPI MoM +0.3% +0.3%
Core CPI YoY +2.5% +2.5%

As mentioned before, the main driver of the surprise is the non-core figure, meaning that the difference is most likely in energy prices. In the past few months, gasoline and other fuels have been falling, while electricity services have been rising.

Here is the top-level report from the BLS:

Pain at the Grocery Store

For those that gauge inflation by the grocery store (a larger group than you may think, and you bet they vote, so this matters a lot), food inflation is outpacing the headline figure still, at 2.9% for the category, but lopsided toward food away from home, which came in at 4% YoY.

This is likely noise and something I have no explanation for, but something is going on in the hog industry. Two of the most volatile figures in food inflation are pork products, with bacon down over 4% MoM and pork chops up over 4% MoM. But the thing that has folks the most hurt at the store is the rapid increase in beef prices, up 12-17% YoY.

All meats seemed to see above-inflation rises, barring pork, which is having that odd moment. Fish in general was up over 5% YoY, with frozen fish at 8.5%. It is no wonder that I keep hearing about how bad inflation is, despite the positive numbers. Groceries is one of the most common ways we gauge price increases. It is looking grim in that category for many currently, even if the headline numbers are much better. A 2.4% headline does not explain that the meats, poultry, and fish category is up 7.0%!

An outlier, due to the bird flu that ravaged the chicken population last year, causing prices to skyrocket, egg prices are down over 35% YoY. They have finally come back to pre-2024 prices in the last few months, and with this change, are now at levels below 2022.

Fuel Prices Highlight Regionality

In fuel, gasoline was down 7.5% YoY, although gas prices are also highly regional, so if your gasoline prices aren't down—mine are not in Southern California—that may be why. Regionality was on full display in this report. The Los Angeles area where I live saw inflation at a rate 25% higher than the reported rate (2.4% vs. 3.0%), but people living in Dallas actually saw a very slight deflation (-0.3%).

Here is the available data by metro:

Shelter Brought The Overall Rate Up

The last major point to highlight from the report is the controversial shelter figure. I've covered before why the calculation the BLS uses for housing payments, “owner's equivalent rent,” or just “OER,” has some issues. It's no different this time around, with a 3.3% increase in OER. Since it has such a high weight in the BLS model, and in consumer wallets to be fair, it was a drag on the overall rate.

Outside the BLS report, rental prices have been declining after having peaked in 2022, although this is anecdotal data from just one rental listing service, so take it with a grain of salt.

That seems to be a continuing trend for now, but it does not translate as cleanly into the OER calculation and also does not account for the prices of homes, which were flat last year across the national average. Although the national average suffers from the same regionality problem we've been discussing, so consider that as well.

Truflation Shows A Rapid Decline

Crypto project and data aggregator Truflation has been attempting to chronicle their inflation gauge. It has chronically undershot the BLS figure, but they argue that their calculations (they do not use OER and such) and weights are more accurate.

While many of my readers are reporting in the comments section of my CPI articles that the BLS numbers are far too low, Truflation is reporting that inflation is now under 1%, having fallen precipitously over the last two months.

Will the Fed React?

My view has consistently been that we will not get another rate cut until June, but that we will see our first cut in June. I argued in the past that if the labor market worsened, that cut could come as early as April, but the last jobs report has sealed the deal for June as the earliest now.

The Fed won't have to worry about committing more resources to getting inflation below the 2% target if it falls there over the coming months. That will clear the way for them to cut, but time is needed to play out.

June is also when we expect to have a new Fed chair for the first time at an FOMC meeting. The current nominee is Kevin Warsh, though it is still unclear if he will be able to get the votes necessary to secure the appointment by the Senate Banking Committee.

Fed funds futures probabilities have risen to 66% for a rate cut, though it is split between 25bp and 50bp lower than today's rate.

Conclusion

The January CPI report was positive and came in just slightly under expectations. The report is a little skewed by fuel and food prices, which have been very volatile. While fuel is down, food is up. Shelter is also up YoY and affecting the overall report, bringing it upwards. Of course, regionality plays a large factor in the U.S., and the report highlights that as well. The report was excellent for Dallas, which actually saw very slight deflation YoY, but that's just how regionality works.

All told, this was a good inflation report. It moves the needle for the Fed, but only slightly, and paves the way for a June rate cut if we continue at this pace. I see little reason for inflation to return currently, barring whatever is going on with the food industry—I know that farmers are having a tough time, but that is for a different article.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: FYLD: Excellent Year And Solid Value For This International ETF
data.description:
This article updates my review of May 2025 in light of current holdings and recent performance.

FYLD strategy

Cambria Foreign Shareholder Yield ETF (FYLD) is an actively managed ETF launched on 12/3/2013, with a portfolio of 99 stocks, a 30-day SEC yield of 3.88%, and a total expense ratio of 0.59%. Dividends are paid quarterly. As described by Cambria, the fund invests in developed markets excluding the U.S. and focuses on shareholder yield, an aggregate of cash dividends, buybacks, and debt paydowns. The portfolio is co-managed by Meb Faber, author of Shareholder Yield, A Better Approach to Dividend Investing.

Cambria selects the top 20% of stocks in the initial universe of developed ex-US, publicly listed companies based on their shareholder yield, as measured by dividend payments and net share buybacks. Cambria’s quantitative algorithm then factors in the remaining stocks’ debt paydowns and applies a number of value metrics to create a composite (...) Cambria then selects the top 100 stocks for inclusion in the Fund’s portfolio that exhibit, in the aggregate, the best combination of shareholder yield characteristics and value metrics.

FYLD tends to an equal-weight methodology, but weighting may vary with market conditions and opportunities. Country weights are capped at 30% of asset value. The portfolio turnover rate was 52% in the most recent fiscal year and 44% in the previous year. This article will use as a benchmark an ex-U.S. developed markets index, represented by iShares Core MSCI International Developed Markets ETF (IDEV).

Shareholder yield backtest

I ran a simulation on Portfolio123 to evaluate the performance of a ranking system based on shareholder yield, calculated as follows:

(Dividend paid TTM + Equity Purchased TTM - Equity Issued TTM + Total Debt 12 months ago - Total Debt)/Market Capitalization

The simulation was run by ranking stocks of the Russell 1000 Index in equal weight in 10 buckets from lower to higher shareholder yields. The next chart plots the total return of the 10 buckets from 1999 to February 2026 with an annual rebalancing (the left orange bar is the cap-weighted Russell 1000 benchmark).

This simulation validates on 27 years of historical data the concept of shareholder yield in the United States. The fund assumes that this bias can be generalized to international markets.

FYLD portfolio

FYLD has about 31% of asset value in large caps, 46% in mid-caps, and 23% in small caps. The heaviest countries in the portfolio are Japan (25.3%), the U.K. (15.3%), and Canada (13.6%). Other countries are below 9%.

The fund is heavily weighted in energy (27%) and has significant exposure in financials (17.6%) and industrials (14.2%). Other sectors are below 10%. Compared to the benchmark, FYLD massively overweights energy, while it ignores healthcare, real estate and downplays mostly technology.

The portfolio is well diversified, with low company-specific risk. The top 10 stock holdings, listed in the next table, represent 12.4% of asset value and the largest position weighs 1.5%.

data.title: TeamViewer: Worsening SMB ARR Growth Bad For The Sales Funnel
data.description:
TeamViewer (TMVWF) reported earnings, reversing the positive effect from a bit ago of the reiterated margin guidance for the FY 2025. The key negatives have been:

  • Margin compression people worried about for 2025 is still happening, but in 2026.
  • Leading indicators of ARR looking poor particularly in SMB which is the top of the TMV sales funnel. They are worsening a little bit in the middle-value deals for SMB compared to our previous coverage.

More generally, the company is contending with a tougher macro environment, more price sensitive SMB customers and also running afoul of AI-based trends such as possibly fewer seats in the per-seat model due to frictional unemployment risks and possibly a less valid thesis around AI possibly replacing TeamViewer and the related IT staff at corporations. FCFYs are closer to 20% at this point at between 180-200 million EUR in FCF against a 780 million EUR market cap. The valuation is compelling, but the SMB trends will mute the next two quarters of 2026 and could make performance of the stock difficult to achieve.

There were positives too this quarter like the conclusion of the launch of TeamViewer ONE (1E DEX and remote session offering combined) as well as some traction with Frontline (the AR offering) that had a notable impact on revenues. We don't see any structural dangers from AI materialising in the way markets may be worrying about for high margin lower-tech businesses like TeamViewer in actuality. Also a slight growth and margin hit still leaves the deleveraging angle intact with the 8 quarter target to return to more normal levels of leverage. That would be the angle we focus on.

Results

TeamViewer made a strategic mistake some quarters back trying to more aggressively monetise the SMB offering, irritating smaller users and landing badly. A tougher macro backdrop compounds the issue. Churns are rising. The issue is that SMB is the top of the funnel.

Negative ARR is really quite a problem because it's a leading indicator. This is going to be weighing on the entire 2026 outlook, where growth expectations are virtually flat despite expectations of decent upsell and traction of TeamViewer one among enterprise customers who will hopefully be eager to get their hands on the more comprehensive DEX-enhanced solution. Obviously, we hope that the autonomous endpoint management idea that was made possible by the 1E acquisition actually pans out.

Also, while margins were maintained as we discussed in our previous coverage for the FY 2025, the compression is getting pushed back into 2026 on weaker growth and also a block on price increases which can no longer land with the customer base, particularly with SMB where declining ARRs is not something the commercial pipeline can actually afford. It will take till the H2 2026 before sequential improvement resumes according to management phasing forecasts.

We do want to note that the growth overall in the current quarter was pretty good driven as always by enterprise. The re was a new deal signed for Frontline, adding possibly around ~5 million EUR in ARR in the Q4 (2% of ARR for enterprise). Sequential ARR growth did occur in Q4 driven by 1E picking up and some major European contract wins adding around 3 million to the ARR. But the NA market for 1E doesn't seem to be that great.

Bottom Line

Possible upside in 2026 could come from unexpected traction of the DEX solution in APAC where 1E was not present before. Also the roll-back of aggressive monetisation of the SMB client base could restore growth there faster than expected. The issue is that the negative exit rate from Q4 is worrying given that's a leading indicator, so there's an additional lag before it's going to be noticed positively in revenues.

But the reality is that the direction is worsening, and will likely stay underwhelming unless TeamViewer ONE gains unexpected traction, which we believe is unlikely given mounting macro pressure and generally low headcount additions. We don't see LLMs as a direct threat to TeamViewer other than the effect on headcounts in corporates in a seat-based model. In the end, IT departments need tools to interact with remote devices to fix problems. DEX and possibly more autonomous endpoint management still has value in an LLM world. We don't see this as a technological bet against AI. But with the direction not imminently improving the story is mainly about low absolute valuation reflected in around 20% FCFY and also the deleveraging angle.

Capital allocation priorities could thereafter change to something like a dividend which would be hefty on the current FCF generation and compressed price. We think the assets could be valued better once leverage ratios fall particularly in the face of lower growth rates, not a great combination but we don't see any major threats to cash generation with the company having made its future proof move already with 1E in our view.
